title: Congee with Century Egg

seo.title: Authentic Century Egg Congee Recipe | Easy Chinese Breakfast

seo.imageAlt: A bowl of creamy Century Egg Congee topped with sliced century egg, green onions, and ginger.
seo.imageCaption: Enjoy a comforting bowl of homemade Century Egg Congee, a traditional Chinese breakfast.

description: Experience the comforting embrace of Century Egg Congee, a beloved Chinese breakfast porridge known for its silky texture and unique savory flavor. This traditional dish, often enjoyed with salted pork or lean meat, offers a deeply satisfying and nourishing start to your day. Our recipe guides you to create a perfectly balanced congee that is both authentic and incredibly delicious.

instructions.title: Prepare the Rice
instructions.text: Rinse the jasmine rice thoroughly under cold water until the water runs clear. For an extra creamy congee, you can soak the rice in water for 30 minutes before cooking, or even freeze it overnight to break down the starch faster.

instructions.title: Start the Congee Base
instructions.text: Combine the rinsed rice and 8 cups of water or chicken broth in a large pot. Bring the mixture to a boil over high heat, then immediately reduce the heat to low. Cover the pot, leaving a small gap for steam to escape, and simmer for at least 30 minutes, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking.

instructions.title: Prepare Century Eggs and Ginger
instructions.text: While the congee simmers, carefully peel the century eggs. Rinse them under cold water to remove any residue, then dice two of the century eggs into small pieces and cut the remaining one or two into wedges for garnish. Peel and julienne the fresh ginger.

instructions.title: Achieve Creamy Consistency
instructions.text: After 30 minutes, check the congee. The rice grains should have broken down and the mixture should be thick and creamy. If it's too thick, add the remaining 2 cups of water or broth, a little at a time, until you reach your desired consistency. Continue to simmer for another 5-10 minutes.

instructions.title: Add Century Eggs and Season
instructions.text: Stir the diced century eggs and most of the julienned ginger into the congee. Season with salt and white pepper to taste. Allow it to simmer for another 2-3 minutes to let the flavors meld. Taste and adjust seasonings as needed.

instructions.title: Serve and Garnish
instructions.text: Ladle the hot Century Egg Congee into individual serving bowls. Garnish each bowl with the remaining julienned ginger, chopped green onions, and the century egg wedges. Drizzle a touch of sesame oil over each serving for extra aroma and flavor. Serve immediately and enjoy!

tips: For the creamiest congee, pre-soak your rice for at least 30 minutes, or even freeze it overnight. Freezing helps break down the rice grains faster during cooking, resulting in a silkier texture.

When selecting century eggs, look for ones with firm whites and a creamy, dark yolk. If you find the flavor too strong, you can blanch the diced century eggs in boiling water for 1-2 minutes before adding to the congee.

Congee thickens as it cools. If you have leftovers, add a splash of water or broth when reheating to restore its desired consistency. Store in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3 days.
article:
**The Ultimate Comfort Bowl: Congee with Century Egg – A Quintessential Chinese Breakfast**

In the vast culinary landscape of China, breakfast is often a symphony of diverse flavors and textures, far removed from the quick cereal and toast of Western culture. Among the myriad options, one dish stands out as a true testament to comfort, tradition, and unique taste: Congee with Century Egg. This creamy, savory rice porridge, often enriched with lean pork, is a beloved staple that nourishes the body and soothes the soul, making it a perfect start to any day. If you're looking to explore authentic Chinese breakfast recipes, diving into the world of **Century Egg Congee** is an essential first step.

## What is Century Egg Congee?

At its heart, congee (or jook) is a simple rice porridge, a dish found in various forms across Asia. It's made by simmering rice in a large amount of water or broth until the grains break down into a thick, creamy consistency. Its simplicity is its strength, serving as a versatile canvas for countless additions. In the case of **Century Egg Congee**, this canvas is adorned with one of Chinese cuisine's most distinctive ingredients: the century egg.

Century eggs, also known as preserved eggs or pidan, are a Chinese delicacy created by preserving duck, chicken, or quail eggs in a mixture of clay, ash, salt, quicklime, and rice hulls for several weeks to several months. The preservation process transforms the egg: the albumen (white) becomes a translucent, dark brown jelly with a pattern resembling pine branches, while the yolk turns dark green to black with a creamy consistency and a strong, complex flavor. Despite their appearance, which can be initially off-putting to the uninitiated, century eggs boast a unique umami richness, a slightly sulfurous aroma, and a smooth, almost custardy texture that is surprisingly delicious when paired correctly.

When diced and stirred into piping hot congee, the century egg melts slightly, imparting its distinctive savory depth and earthy notes throughout the creamy rice. Often, lean pork, thinly sliced or minced, is added to provide another layer of texture and flavor, resulting in a hearty, flavorful, and incredibly comforting bowl that is both satisfying and deeply traditional.

## Ingredients for Perfect Century Egg Congee

Crafting a truly exceptional bowl of **Century Egg Congee** requires a few key ingredients, each playing a crucial role in the final flavor and texture profile.

*   **Rice:** Long-grain jasmine rice is commonly used, as it breaks down beautifully to create a creamy texture. Some prefer a mix of long-grain and short-grain for a slight chewiness.
*   **Liquid:** While water can be used, chicken or pork broth will significantly enhance the depth of flavor. Aim for a ratio of about 1 cup of rice to 8-10 cups of liquid for a creamy consistency.
*   **Century Eggs:** You'll need 2-3 century eggs per cup of dry rice. Peel them carefully, rinse, and dice them into small, bite-sized pieces.
*   **Lean Pork (Optional but Recommended):** About 4-6 ounces of thinly sliced pork loin or minced pork shoulder per cup of rice. Marinate it with a pinch of salt, white pepper, a dash of soy sauce, and a teaspoon of cornstarch to keep it tender.
*   **Ginger:** Fresh ginger is essential. Thinly sliced or julienned, it adds a warm, pungent aroma that balances the century egg's richness.
*   **Seasonings:** Salt and white pepper are the primary seasonings. A splash of sesame oil at the end adds a nutty aroma.
*   **Garnishes (Optional):** Chopped green onions, fresh cilantro, fried shallots, or a drizzle of chili oil elevate the dish further.

## Step-by-Step Instructions to Make Congee

Making congee is more about patience than complex technique. Follow these steps for a perfect bowl of **Century Egg Congee**:

1.  **Prepare the Rice:** Rinse 1 cup of rice thoroughly under cold water until the water runs clear. For an even creamier congee that cooks faster, soak the rinsed rice in water for at least 30 minutes, or even overnight. Some also recommend freezing the wet rice for a few hours, which helps the grains shatter more easily during cooking.
2.  **Start the Congee Base:** In a large, heavy-bottomed pot, combine the rinsed (and optionally soaked/frozen) rice with 8-10 cups of water or broth and a few slices of fresh ginger. Bring the mixture to a rolling boil over high heat, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking.
3.  **Simmer and Stir:** Once boiling, reduce the heat to the lowest setting, cover the pot partially (leaving a small gap for steam to escape), and let it simmer gently for 1 to 1.5 hours. Stir every 15-20 minutes, scraping the bottom of the pot to prevent the rice from sticking and burning. The rice grains should gradually break down, and the liquid should thicken into a creamy porridge. If it becomes too thick, add a little more hot water or broth.
4.  **Prepare Pork (if using):** While the congee simmers, marinate your thinly sliced or minced pork with the seasonings mentioned above. Once the congee is nearly ready, you can blanch the pork quickly in a separate pot of boiling water for about 30 seconds until just cooked, or stir-fry it briefly until no longer pink. This ensures the pork remains tender and doesn't get tough from overcooking in the congee.
5.  **Add Century Eggs and Pork:** Once the congee has reached your desired creamy consistency, stir in the diced century eggs and the cooked pork (if using). Continue to simmer for another 5-10 minutes, allowing the flavors to meld and the century eggs to warm through.
6.  **Season and Serve:** Remove the ginger slices. Season the congee generously with salt and white pepper to taste. Ladle the hot congee into individual bowls. Garnish with chopped green onions, cilantro, and a drizzle of sesame oil. Serve immediately.

## Tips for the Best Century Egg Congee

Achieving that perfect, silky smooth texture and rich flavor in your congee is easier with a few insider tips:

*   **Ratio is Key:** The 1:8 or 1:10 rice-to-liquid ratio is crucial for creamy congee. Adjust to your preferred thickness.
*   **Low and Slow:** Patience is a virtue when making congee. A long, gentle simmer is essential for the rice grains to fully break down and release their starch, creating that signature creamy texture.
*   **Don't Skimp on Broth:** Using good quality chicken or pork broth instead of just water will significantly deepen the flavor profile of your congee.
*   **Fresh Ginger Matters:** Fresh ginger not only adds a lovely aroma but also helps to balance the unique, rich flavor of the century egg.
*   **Add Century Eggs Last:** Don't add the century eggs too early in the cooking process. Overcooking them can diminish their distinct texture and flavor. Stir them in during the last 5-10 minutes.
*   **Stir Often:** Especially in the beginning and towards the end, frequent stirring prevents the rice from sticking to the bottom of the pot and ensures even cooking.

## Serving Suggestions & Pairings

**Century Egg Congee** is a complete meal in itself, particularly for breakfast or a light lunch. However, it's often enjoyed with various accompaniments that add texture and flavor contrasts.

*   **Youtiao (Chinese Fried Dough Sticks):** These crispy, airy fried dough sticks are perhaps the most classic pairing. Dip them into the hot congee to soak up the savory broth.
*   **Pickled Vegetables:** A small side of pickled radish, mustard greens, or fermented tofu provides a tangy, salty counterpoint to the creamy congee.
*   **Soy Sauce and Chili Oil:** For those who like an extra kick, a drizzle of soy sauce or a spoonful of homemade chili oil can be added directly to the bowl.
*   **Roasted Peanuts:** A sprinkle of crunchy roasted peanuts adds a delightful textural contrast.
*   **Other Dim Sum:** Congee often appears on dim sum menus and can be enjoyed alongside other favorites like steamed dumplings or spring rolls.

## Variations and Add-ins

While **Century Egg Congee** is delicious in its traditional form, congee is incredibly versatile. Don't hesitate to experiment with these variations and add-ins:

*   **Other Meats:** Instead of or in addition to pork, try shredded chicken, thinly sliced fish (often added raw and cooked by the heat of the congee), or even salted duck meat for a different flavor profile.
*   **Salted Duck Egg:** Often served alongside century egg, a salted duck egg adds another layer of salty, rich flavor and a contrasting texture. You can crumble it into the congee or serve it on the side.
*   **Vegetables:** For added nutrients and flavor, stir in finely diced carrots, mushrooms, or spinach during the last 15 minutes of cooking.
*   **Herbs and Spices:** Experiment with different herbs like more cilantro, or a pinch of white pepper for a subtle spice. A tiny dash of fish sauce can also add another layer of umami.
*   **Garnishes Galore:** Beyond green onions and cilantro, consider crispy fried garlic, roasted sesame seeds, or even a few drops of light soy sauce for an extra savory punch.

Embracing the world of Chinese breakfast means discovering dishes like **Century Egg Congee** – a truly unique and deeply comforting experience. Its rich history, distinctive flavors, and nourishing qualities make it a standout dish that is well worth the effort to prepare. So, next time you're craving something warm, savory, and wonderfully authentic, gather your ingredients and embark on a culinary journey to create this beloved Chinese classic. Your taste buds will thank you.
